revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を…」
revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）、大学に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは性識聡慧（せいしきそうけい）にして、藝業優長（げいぎょうゆうちょう）なる者、十人以下五人以上の専（たく）め学問に精（いそ）しきを選び、善き誘（いざない）を加へむことを。仍（より）て、夏・冬の服、幷せて食料（じきりょう）を賜はむ」として、[[大学寮]]に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。また、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるように命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に医得業生が設置されている。
以後、紀伝得業生、大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。このうち、文章得業生は[[弘仁]]11年（[[820年]]）には秀才生と改称されたが、[[天長]]4年（[[827年]]）には元の名称にもどされている。数年の勉学の後、[[対策]]に及第して任官するのが普通であったが、平安中期までは、対策を受けないまま地方官などになる場合もあった。
文章得業生が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『岩波日本史辞典』p836、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『続日本紀』3 新日本古典文学大系14 岩波書店、1990年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とくこうしよう}} [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]] [[Category:大学寮]]

contributor.username: ja>壁掛成蛸は三考する葦である

revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）、大学に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは性識聡慧（せいしきそうけい）にして、藝業優長（げいぎょうゆうちょう）なる者、十人以下五人以上の専（たく）め学問に精（いそ）しきを選び、善き誘（いざない）を加へむことを。仍（より）て、夏・冬の服、幷せて食料（じきりょう）を賜はむ」として、[[大学寮]]に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。また、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるように命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に医得業生が設置されている。
以後、紀伝得業生、大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。このうち、文章得業生は[[弘仁]]11年（[[820年]]）には秀才生と改称されたが、[[天長]]4年（[[827年]]）には元の名称にもどされている。数年の勉学の後、[[対策]]に及第して任官するのが普通であったが、平安中期までは、対策を受けないまま地方官などになる場合もあった。
文章得業生が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『岩波日本史辞典』p836、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『続日本紀』3 新日本古典文学大系14 岩波書店、1990年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Education-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とくこうしよう}} [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]] [[Category:大学寮]]

revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）3月、[[太政官]]は以下のような奏上を行った。
[[大学寮|大学]]に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは性識聡慧（せいしきそうけい）にして、藝業優長（げいぎょうゆうちょう）なる者、十人以下五人以上の専（たく）め学問に精（いそ）しきを選び、善き誘（いざない）を加へむことを。仍（より）て、夏・冬の服、幷せて食料（じきりょう）を賜はむ」として、大学寮に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。同じ奏上では、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるように命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に医得業生が設置されている。
以後、紀伝得業生、大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。このうち、文章得業生は[[弘仁]]11年（[[820年]]）には秀才生と改称されたが、[[天長]]4年（[[827年]]）には元の名称にもどされている。数年の勉学の後、[[対策]]に及第して任官するのが普通であったが、平安中期までは、対策を受けないまま地方官などになる場合もあった。
文章得業生が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『岩波日本史辞典』p836、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『続日本紀』3 新日本古典文学大系14 岩波書店、1990年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年

revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことで、[[大学寮]]・[[典薬寮]]・[[陰陽寮]]に設置された上級のコースである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）3月、[[太政官]]は以下のような奏上を行った。
[[大学寮|大学]]に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは性識聡慧（せいしきそうけい）にして、藝業優長（げいぎょうゆうちょう）なる者、十人以下五人以上の専（たく）め学問に精（いそ）しきを選び、善き誘（いざない）を加へむことを。仍（より）て、夏・冬の服、幷せて食料（じきりょう）を賜はむ」として、大学寮に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。同じ奏上では、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるように命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]<ref>『延喜式』巻16「陰陽寮」10</ref>・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に医得業生が設置されている。
ここで重要なのは、大学の得業生は成績優秀な学生を推挙するためであり、陰陽寮・典薬寮の場合は後継者育成のためと、目的が異なっている。ただし、ともに[[式部省]]施行の貢挙に対応するものではなかった。
以後、紀伝得業生、[[承和]]12年（[[845年]]）以前には大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。この間に得業生は貢挙受験コースと見なされるようになった。[[延喜]]13年（[[913年]]）には、貢挙受験は得業生を原則とすることになり、「得業生試」と呼ばれ、この試自体が専門の学者になるための過程とされるようになった、という。
[[文章得業生]]が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『岩波日本史辞典』p836、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『国史大辞典』十一巻p303、文：[[久木幸男]]、[[吉川弘文館]]、1989年 * 『続日本紀』3 新日本古典文学大系14 岩波書店、1990年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Education-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とくこうしよう}} [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]] [[Category:大学寮]]

revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことで、[[大学寮]]・[[典薬寮]]・[[陰陽寮]]に設置された上級のコースである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）3月、[[太政官]]は以下のような奏上を行った。
[[大学寮|大学]]に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは性識聡慧（せいしきそうけい）にして、藝業優長（げいぎょうゆうちょう）なる者、十人以下五人以上の専（たく）め学問に精（いそ）しきを選び、善き誘（いざない）を加へむことを。仍（より）て、夏・冬の服、幷せて食料（じきりょう）を賜はむ」として、大学寮に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。同じ奏上では、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるようにと命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]<ref>『延喜式』巻16「陰陽寮」10</ref>・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に[[医得業生]]が設置されている。
ここで重要なのは、大学の得業生は成績優秀な学生を推挙するためであり、陰陽寮・典薬寮の場合は後継者育成のためと、目的が異なっている。ただし、ともに[[式部省]]施行の貢挙に対応するものではなかった。
以後、紀伝得業生、[[承和]]12年（[[845年]]）以前には大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。この間に得業生は式部省の貢挙受験コースと見なされるようになった。[[延喜]]13年（[[913年]]）には、貢挙受験は得業生を原則とすることになり、「得業生試」と呼ばれ、この試自体が専門の学者になるための過程とされるようになった、という。
[[文章得業生]]が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことで、[[大学寮]]・[[典薬寮]]・[[陰陽寮]]に設置された上級のコースである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）3月、[[太政官]]は以下のような奏上を行った。
[[大学寮|大学]]に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは{{読み仮名|性識聡慧|せいしきそうけい}}にして、{{読み仮名|藝業優長|げいぎょうゆうちょう}}なる者、十人以下五人以上の{{読み仮名|専|たく}}め学問に{{読み仮名|精|いそ}}しきを選び、善き{{読み仮名|誘|いざない}}を加へむことを。{{読み仮名|仍|より}}て、夏・冬の服、幷せて{{読み仮名|食料|じきりょう}}を賜はむ」として、大学寮に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。同じ奏上では、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるようにと命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]<ref>『延喜式』巻16「陰陽寮」10</ref>・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に[[医得業生]]が設置されている。
ここで重要なのは、大学の得業生は成績優秀な学生を推挙するためであり、陰陽寮・典薬寮の場合は後継者育成のためと、目的が異なっている。ただし、ともに[[式部省]]施行の貢挙に対応するものではなかった。
以後、紀伝得業生、[[承和 (日本)|承和]]12年（[[845年]]）以前には大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。この間に得業生は式部省の貢挙受験コースと見なされるようになった。[[延喜]]13年（[[913年]]）には、貢挙受験は得業生を原則とすることになり、「得業生試」と呼ばれ、この試自体が専門の学者になるための過程とされるようになった、という。
[[文章得業生]]が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『岩波日本史辞典』p836、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『国史大辞典』十一巻p303、文：[[久木幸男]]、[[吉川弘文館]]、1989年 *『日本古代史事典』p373、[[遠藤元男]]：編、[[朝倉書店]]、1973年 * 『続日本紀』3 新日本古典文学大系14 岩波書店、1990年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年

revision.text:
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Education-stub}}

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.

By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}

[[Category:Systems in Japanese history]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:Daigakuryō]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokugōshō-qq]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokugōshō-qq]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Tokugōshō]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:得業生]]

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

[[Category:Systems in Japanese history]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:Daigakuryō]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokugōshō-qq]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tokugōshō-qq]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Tokugōshō]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:得業生]]

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

[[Category:Systems in Japanese history]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:Daigakuryō]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tokugōshō-qq]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tokugōshō-qq]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Tokugōshō]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:得業生]]

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.

[[Category:Systems in Japanese history]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:Daigakuryō]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] <!--Tokugōshō-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] <!--Tokugōshō-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Tokugōshō]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokugōshō]][[ja:得業生]]

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. == Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."

The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

revision.text:
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}

[[Category:Systems in Japanese history]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:Daigakuryō]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Tokugōshō-->[[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Tokugōshō-->[[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Tokugōshō]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:okugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tokugōshō]][[ja:得業生]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokugōshō}}

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|天文道}}" to "|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}}"
revision.text:
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}

[[Category:Systems in Japanese history]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:Daigakuryō]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] <!--Tokugōshō-->[[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] <!--Tokugōshō-->[[Category:Tokugōshō]][[Category:Tokugōshō]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:okugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tokugōshō]][[ja:得業生]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Tokugōshō]][[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokugōshō}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion tier 2]][[Category: and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}

revision.text:
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平|12=draft}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官|12=draft}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=draft}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=draft}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=draft}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=draft}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=draft}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|12=draft}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=draft}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=draft}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|12=draft}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和|12=draft}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion tier 2]]
<!--Tokugōshō-->
<!--Tokugōshō-->
{{draft categories| [[Category: and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜|12=draft}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=draft}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店|12=draft}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=draft}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店|12=draft}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=draft}}, 1992.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Education-stub}}

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平|12=draft}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijōkan|ja|太政官|12=draft}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=draft}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=draft}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=draft}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=draft}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=draft}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|12=draft}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=draft}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=draft}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|12=draft}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和|12=draft}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi|ja|延喜|12=draft}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=draft}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}.
