revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことで、[[大学寮]]・[[典薬寮]]・[[陰陽寮]]に設置された上級のコースである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）3月、[[太政官]]は以下のような奏上を行った。
[[大学寮|大学]]に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは{{読み仮名|性識聡慧|せいしきそうけい}}にして、{{読み仮名|藝業優長|げいぎょうゆうちょう}}なる者、十人以下五人以上の{{読み仮名|専|たく}}め学問に{{読み仮名|精|いそ}}しきを選び、善き{{読み仮名|誘|いざない}}を加へむことを。{{読み仮名|仍|より}}て、夏・冬の服、幷せて{{読み仮名|食料|じきりょう}}を賜はむ」として、大学寮に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。同じ奏上では、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるようにと命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]<ref>『延喜式』巻16「陰陽寮」10</ref>・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に[[医得業生]]が設置されている。
ここで重要なのは、大学の得業生は成績優秀な学生を推挙するためであり、陰陽寮・典薬寮の場合は後継者育成のためと、目的が異なっている。ただし、ともに[[式部省]]施行の貢挙に対応するものではなかった。
以後、紀伝得業生、[[承和 (日本)|承和]]12年（[[845年]]）以前には大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。この間に得業生は式部省の貢挙受験コースと見なされるようになった。[[延喜]]13年（[[913年]]）には、貢挙受験は得業生を原則とすることになり、「得業生試」と呼ばれ、この試自体が専門の学者になるための過程とされるようになった、という。
[[文章得業生]]が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『岩波日本史辞典』p836、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『国史大辞典』十一巻p303、文：[[久木幸男]]、[[吉川弘文館]]、1989年 *『日本古代史事典』p373、[[遠藤元男]]：編、[[朝倉書店]]、1973年 * 『続日本紀』3 新日本古典文学大系14 岩波書店、1990年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Education-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とくこうしよう}} [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]] [[Category:大学寮]]

revision.text:
'''得業生'''（とくごうしょう）とは、古代の学制で、各学科において一般[[学生]]よりも高度な学問・技術の習得を求められた特待生身分のことで、[[大学寮]]・[[典薬寮]]・[[陰陽寮]]に設置された上級のコースである。 == 概要 == [[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）3月、[[太政官]]は以下のような奏上を行った。
[[大学寮|大学]]に在籍する学生の中には、年月を経ても学業を学ぶことが浅薄なもの、好学なのに家が貧しくて学資を十分に払えないものもいるので、「望み請はくは{{読み仮名|性識聡慧|せいしきそうけい}}にして、{{読み仮名|藝業優長|げいぎょうゆうちょう}}なる者、十人以下五人以上の{{読み仮名|専|たく}}め学問に{{読み仮名|精|いそ}}しきを選び、善き{{読み仮名|誘|いざない}}を加へむことを。{{読み仮名|仍|より}}て、夏・冬の服、幷せて{{読み仮名|食料|じきりょう}}を賜はむ」として、大学寮に[[明経道]]4人・[[紀伝道|文章道]]2人・[[明法道]]2人・[[算道]]2人の各得業生が選ばれた。同じ奏上では、諸博士が高齢で老衰してきたことから学業を後進に教授することになり、[[吉宜|吉田宜]]・[[大津首]]・[[谷那庚受|難波清成]]・[[山口田主]]・[[私部石村]]・[[志斐三田次]]らが、時服・食料は大学生に準えて、陰陽・医術にそれぞれ3人、曜・暦に各2人の弟子を取って学ばせるようにと命ぜられている<ref>『続日本紀』天平2年3月27日条</ref>。これにより、[[陰陽寮]]に[[陰陽道]]・[[天文道]]<ref>『延喜式』巻16「陰陽寮」10</ref>・[[暦道]]の各得業生、[[典薬寮]]に[[医得業生]]が設置されている。
ここで重要なのは、大学の得業生は成績優秀な学生を推挙するためであり、陰陽寮・典薬寮の場合は後継者育成のためと、目的が異なっている。ただし、ともに[[式部省]]施行の貢挙に対応するものではなかった。
以後、紀伝得業生、[[承和 (日本)|承和]]12年（[[845年]]）以前には大宰府得業生も設置され、一定期間の修学後、試験により修了を認定されて、各道の教官その他の専門の官職に就いた。この間に得業生は式部省の貢挙受験コースと見なされるようになった。[[延喜]]13年（[[913年]]）には、貢挙受験は得業生を原則とすることになり、「得業生試」と呼ばれ、この試自体が専門の学者になるための過程とされるようになった、という。
[[文章得業生]]が室町初期まで存続した以外は平安後期に消滅した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『岩波日本史辞典』p836、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『国史大辞典』十一巻p303、文：[[久木幸男]]、[[吉川弘文館]]、1989年 *『日本古代史事典』p373、[[遠藤元男]]：編、[[朝倉書店]]、1973年 * 『続日本紀』3 新日本古典文学大系14 岩波書店、1990年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Education-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とくこうしよう}} [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]] [[Category:大学寮]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97212787]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== Overview ==

In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:

Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|qq=|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|qq=|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|qq=|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|qq=|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|qq=|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>

The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.

Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|qq=|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

== See Also ==
* [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]
* {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}

{{Japanese-history-stub}}

* ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|qq=|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989.
* ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|qq=|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|qq=|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973.
* ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990.
* ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|qq=|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992.
{{Education-stub}}

'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== Overview ==

In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:

Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>

The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.

Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata no short description - based on Wikidata English short description
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q97212787]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== Overview ==

In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:

Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>

The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.

Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

== See Also ==
* [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]
* {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}

{{Japanese-history-stub}}

* ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989.
* ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973.
* ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990.
* ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992.
{{Education-stub}}

[[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q97212787]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== Overview ==

In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:

Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>

The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.

Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q97212787]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== Overview ==

In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:

Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>

The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.

Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

== See Also ==
* [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]
* {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== See Also == * [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}
}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q97212787]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== See Also == * [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}
}}

Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== Overview ==

In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:

Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>

This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>

The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.

Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.

Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q97212787]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== See Also == * [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}
}}

== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}

{{wikidata link|Q97212787}} [[Category:Ancient Japanese institutions]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:University Dormitories]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97212787]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== See Also == * [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97212787]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== See Also == * [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}
}}

As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== See Also == * [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}
}}
