{{wikidata link|Q97212787}} [[Category:Ancient Japanese institutions]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of education in Japan]] [[Category:University Dormitories]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97212787]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] 2 (730), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]], some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''[[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]]'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa]] 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By [[:en:Engi (era)|Engi]] 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], most of these programs disappeared by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== See Also == * [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}

== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}

It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|en|Jōwa|WD=Q3577279|lt=Jōwa}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|WD=Q1374325|lt=Engi}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97212787]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|en|Jōwa|WD=Q3577279|lt=Jōwa}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|WD=Q1374325|lt=Engi}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}
}}
{{translated page|ja|得業生|version=99494017|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]]
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. == See Also == * {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]]
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Ōtsu no Omi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Tanina no Muraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|en|Jōwa|WD=Q3577279|lt=Jōwa}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|WD=Q1374325|lt=Engi}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'', p. 836, edited by {{ill|Nagahara Keiji|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahara Keiji|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549910}}, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1999. * ''Kokushi Daijiten'', vol. 11, p. 303, by {{ill|Kuki Yukio|ja|久木幸男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Yukio|1=Yukio Hisaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369404}}, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1989. * ''Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten'', p. 373, edited by {{ill|Endō Mototsugu|ja|遠藤元男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endō Mototsugu|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642188}}, {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Shoten|1=Asakura Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11517313}}, 1973. * ''Shoku Nihongi'', vol. 3, New Japanese Classical Literature Series, vol. 14, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1990. * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Complete Modern Translation, vol. 1), Kodansha Academic Library, translated by {{ill|Ujitani Osamu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Osamu|1=Tsutomu Ujitani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449517}}, 1992. {{Education-stub}}
}}

Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō|1=Daigaku-ryō}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Q51574202|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Q65283033|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|1=Shikibu-shō}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|en|Jōwa|WD=Q3577279|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|WD=Q1374325|lt=Engi|1=Engi (era)}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō|1=Daigaku-ryō}} * {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} * {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:得業生]]
'''Tokugōshō''' (得業生) refers to the status of scholarship students in ancient Japanese educational systems, who were expected to acquire advanced academic knowledge and technical skills beyond those of general [[:en:Students|Students]]. It was an upper-level course established in institutions such as {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō|1=Daigaku-ryō}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
In {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} 2 (730), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan|1=Daijō-kan}} submitted the following proposal:
Among the students enrolled in the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō|1=Daigaku-ryō}}, some lacked sufficient progress despite their extended study periods, while others, despite their strong academic inclinations, struggled financially to cover their tuition. "We propose selecting students with exceptional talent ({{ill|性識聡慧|ja|せいしきそうけい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/性識聡慧|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and superior skills ({{ill|藝業優長|ja|げいぎょうゆうちょう|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/藝業優長|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})—a group of no fewer than five and no more than ten—for dedicated study and providing them with summer and winter clothing as well as rations."
As a result, the Daigakuryō selected {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meikyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11241198}} (Confucian studies) students (4), {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604971}} (historical studies) students (2), {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōdō|1=Myōbōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512427}} (law) students (2), and {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandomyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addressed the aging of various instructors, requiring them to teach their expertise to successors. The proposal instructed {{ill|Yoshinori|ja|吉田宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉宜|1=Kichi no Yoroshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990341}}, {{ill|Ōtsu no Omi|ja|大津首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu no Omi|1=Q51574202|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q51574202}}, {{ill|Tanina no Muraji|ja|谷那庚受|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanina no Muraji|1=Q65283033|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283033}}, and others to take disciples in fields such as astronomy, medicine, and calendrical studies, with provisions mirroring those for university students.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Tenpyō 2, March 27th entry.</ref>
This initiative led to the establishment of ''Tokugōshō'' specializing in {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}, {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} within the Onmyōryō, as well as ''Tokugōshō'' in medicine within the Ten'yakuryō.<ref>''{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}'', vol. 16, "Onmyōryō."</ref>
The key distinction lies in the purposes of these programs: ''Tokugōshō'' at the university aimed to promote academic excellence among students, whereas those in the Onmyōryō and Ten'yakuryō were intended for training successors. Both systems were not directly related to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|1=Shikibu-shō}}'s formal civil service examinations.
Subsequently, ''Kiden Tokugōshō'' and ''Dazaifu Tokugōshō'' (before {{ill|Jōwa|en|Jōwa|WD=Q3577279|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa}} 12, 845) were established, with graduates undergoing exams to certify their completion and then assuming teaching or specialized governmental positions. Over time, ''Tokugōshō'' became viewed as a preparatory course for the Shikibu-shō's examinations. By {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|WD=Q1374325|lt=Engi|1=Engi (era)}} 13 (913), participation in ''Tokugōshō'' became a prerequisite for examination eligibility, and the system itself evolved into a pathway for becoming a scholar in specialized fields.
Except for {{ill|Bungaku Tokugōshō|ja|文章得業生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungaku Tokugōshō|1=monjoutokugoushou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378716}}, which survived into the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, most of these programs disappeared by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
